title: Life Processes
summary: Chapter 5 covers the fundamental life processes of living organisms: nutrition, respiration, transportation, and excretion. Photosynthesis in plants and the digestive system in humans are studied in detail. Aerobic and anaerobic respiration are distinguished. The human circulatory system including the heart, blood vessels, and blood are explained. Excretion in humans (kidneys) and in plants is also covered.
learningPoints: Define life processes: nutrition, respiration, transportation, excretion
Explain photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ (in presence of sunlight and chlorophyll)
Describe the events of photosynthesis: light absorption, water splitting, CO₂ reduction
Explain the human digestive system with diagram — mouth to anus
Name digestive enzymes: amylase (salivary), pepsin (gastric), trypsin (pancreatic), lipase
Distinguish aerobic respiration from anaerobic respiration
Write equation for aerobic respiration: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + Energy (ATP)
Explain anaerobic respiration in yeast and muscles
Describe the human heart with diagram — four chambers, double circulation
Explain the role of xylem and phloem in plant transportation
Describe the human kidney and nephron — filtration, reabsorption, urine formation
Name nitrogenous wastes: urea, uric acid, creatinine

laws.name: Photosynthesis Equation
laws.statement: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + Light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Occurs in chloroplasts. Chlorophyll absorbs light. Water splits to provide H⁺. CO₂ is reduced to glucose.

laws.name: Aerobic Respiration
laws.statement: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + Energy (38 ATP). Occurs in mitochondria. Complete oxidation of glucose.

formulas.label: Photosynthesis
formulas.description: In presence of sunlight and chlorophyll. Produces glucose and oxygen.

formulas.label: Aerobic Respiration
formulas.description: Complete oxidation of glucose in mitochondria. 38 ATP produced.

formulas.label: Anaerobic Respiration (Yeast)
formulas.description: In absence of O₂ in yeast: glucose → ethanol + CO₂. Used in fermentation.

formulas.label: Anaerobic Respiration (Muscles)
formulas.description: During vigorous exercise, muscles convert glucose to lactic acid causing cramps.

diagrams.title: Human Digestive System
diagrams.description: Mouth → Oesophagus → Stomach → Small intestine (duodenum, jejunum, ileum) → Large intestine → Rectum → Anus. Label liver, pancreas, gallbladder.

diagrams.title: Schematic Sectional View of Human Heart
diagrams.description: Four chambers: Right atrium, Right ventricle, Left atrium, Left ventricle. Septum divides left and right. Valves: tricuspid (right) and bicuspid (left). Aorta exits left ventricle; pulmonary artery exits right ventricle.

diagrams.title: Structure of Nephron
diagrams.description: Glomerulus (filtration) → Bowman's capsule → PCT → Loop of Henle → DCT → Collecting duct. Reabsorption occurs in tubules.

resources.label: Karnataka SSLC Science Textbook
resources.description: Official Karnataka SSLC Science textbook. Chapter 5 covers Life Processes.

resources.label: NCERT Science Textbook Class 10
resources.description: NCERT Class 10 Science Chapter 6 Life Processes.

resources.label: Life Processes — Video Lessons
resources.description: Khan Academy videos on photosynthesis, respiration, circulation and excretion.

pyqs.question: An enzyme secreted by the pancreas to digest protein is: (A) amylase (B) pepsin (C) trypsin (D) lipase
pyqs.answer: (C) trypsin. Trypsin is a proteolytic enzyme secreted by the pancreas. Pepsin is secreted by stomach.

pyqs.question: What is the importance of xylem tissue in plants?
pyqs.answer: Xylem carries water and dissolved minerals from the roots to other parts of the plant (unidirectional flow). It provides structural support to the plant.

pyqs.question: Name: (i) Structures that prevent collapsing of trachea. (ii) Nitrogenous chemicals separated from blood by kidney.
pyqs.answer: (i) Rings of cartilage prevent collapsing of trachea. (ii) Urea and uric acid (also creatinine).

pyqs.question: In which form is excess glucose stored in plants and animals?
pyqs.answer: In plants: starch. In animals: glycogen (stored mainly in liver and muscles).

pyqs.question: The correct statement regarding bile juice is: (A) secreted by stomach and acidifies food (B) secreted by stomach and alkalises food (C) secreted by liver and alkalises food (D) secreted by pancreas and alkalises food
pyqs.answer: (C) Bile juice is secreted by the liver (stored in gallbladder) and it alkalises food. It emulsifies fats.

pyqs.question: What is the relationship between amount of haemoglobin and ATP released?
pyqs.answer: As amount of haemoglobin increases, oxygen supply to cells increases. More O₂ → more aerobic respiration → more ATP released.

pyqs.question: What are the functions of aorta and pulmonary veins?
pyqs.answer: Aorta: carries oxygenated blood from heart (left ventricle) to all parts of the body. Pulmonary vein: carries oxygenated blood from lungs to heart (left atrium).

pyqs.question: Draw a diagram showing the schematic sectional view of the human heart. Label Septum and Right atrium.
pyqs.answer: Diagram of heart with: Right atrium (receives deoxygenated blood from body), Left atrium (receives oxygenated blood from lungs), Right ventricle (pumps to lungs), Left ventricle (pumps to body), Septum (divides left and right sides).

mcqs.question: An enzyme secreted by the pancreas to digest protein is:
mcqs.explanation: Pancreas secretes trypsin (protein), lipase (fat), amylase (starch). Pepsin is secreted by stomach.

mcqs.question: The correct statement about bile juice is:
options: A. Secreted by stomach, acidifies food
B. Secreted by stomach, alkalises food
C. Secreted by liver, alkalises food
D. Secreted by pancreas, alkalises food
mcqs.explanation: Bile is secreted by liver, stored in gallbladder, alkalises acidic food from stomach, emulsifies fats.

mcqs.question: Structures present in human small intestine to absorb digested food are:
mcqs.explanation: Villi (finger-like projections) in small intestine increase surface area for absorption of digested food.

mcqs.question: In which form is excess glucose stored in animals?
mcqs.explanation: Animals store excess glucose as glycogen in liver and muscles. Plants store it as starch.

mcqs.question: The equation for anaerobic respiration in yeast is:
mcqs.explanation: In yeast (anaerobic): C₆H₁₂O₆ → 2C₂H₅OH + 2CO₂. Used in fermentation and making bread.

mcqs.question: Nitrogenous chemicals separated from blood by kidneys are:
mcqs.explanation: Kidneys filter urea, uric acid, creatinine from blood. These are formed from protein metabolism.

mcqs.question: Aorta carries blood from:
options: B. Left ventricle to all body parts
D. Right atrium to right ventricle
mcqs.explanation: Aorta = largest artery. Carries oxygenated blood from left ventricle to all parts of body.

mcqs.question: Photosynthesis occurs in:
mcqs.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll which absorbs light for photosynthesis. Mitochondria are for respiration.

mcqs.question: Which blood vessel carries deoxygenated blood from body to heart?
mcqs.explanation: Vena cava (superior and inferior) brings deoxygenated blood from body to right atrium of heart.

mcqs.question: Transpiration in plants occurs mainly through:
mcqs.explanation: Stomata are tiny pores in leaves through which water vapour is lost (transpiration) and gas exchange occurs.

mcqs.question: Filtration in nephron occurs at:
mcqs.explanation: Blood is filtered under pressure at glomerulus → filtrate collects in Bowman's capsule. Proteins and blood cells are not filtered.

mcqs.question: The rings of cartilage in trachea prevent:
mcqs.explanation: C-shaped cartilage rings keep trachea open and prevent it from collapsing during breathing.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is NOT a life process?
mcqs.explanation: Life processes are: nutrition, respiration, transportation, excretion, growth, reproduction. Locomotion is NOT essential for life.

mcqs.question: Phloem transports:
options: D. Oxygen from leaves to roots
mcqs.explanation: Phloem transports food (glucose, sucrose) from leaves to all parts bidirectionally. Xylem transports water unidirectionally upward.

mcqs.question: During vigorous exercise, muscles produce lactic acid because:
options: B. Glucose cannot be broken down
C. Anaerobic respiration occurs due to lack of O₂
mcqs.explanation: During vigorous exercise, O₂ supply is insufficient. Anaerobic respiration: glucose → lactic acid. Lactic acid causes muscle cramps.

exercises.question: Explain photosynthesis with its events. Write the overall equation.
exercises.answer: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ (in chloroplasts, with sunlight and chlorophyll). Events: (i) Absorption of light by chlorophyll (ii) Conversion of light energy to chemical energy (iii) Splitting of water molecules into H⁺ and O₂ (light reaction) (iv) Reduction of CO₂ to glucose using H⁺ (dark reaction/Calvin cycle).

exercises.question: Draw a labelled diagram of the human heart. Describe double circulation.
exercises.answer: Heart has 4 chambers: 2 atria + 2 ventricles. Right side: deoxygenated blood → lungs (pulmonary circulation). Left side: oxygenated blood → body (systemic circulation). Double circulation ensures O₂-rich blood doesn't mix with deoxygenated blood. Septum prevents mixing.

exercises.question: Describe the pathway of food through the human digestive system.
exercises.answer: Mouth (amylase→starch) → Oesophagus (peristalsis) → Stomach (pepsin+HCl→protein) → Small intestine (trypsin+lipase → protein+fat; bile emulsifies fat; villi absorb nutrients) → Large intestine (water absorption) → Rectum → Anus.

exercises.question: Explain urine formation in the nephron with diagram.
exercises.answer: (i) Filtration: Blood filtered at glomerulus; water, urea, salts, glucose pass to Bowman's capsule (ii) Reabsorption: Useful substances (glucose, amino acids, water, salts) reabsorbed in tubules back to blood (iii) Secretion: Additional wastes secreted into tubule (iv) Concentrated urine passes to collecting duct → ureter → bladder → urethra.

exercises.question: Compare aerobic and anaerobic respiration.
exercises.answer: Aerobic: requires O₂, occurs in mitochondria, complete oxidation of glucose, produces 38 ATP, products CO₂+H₂O. Anaerobic: no O₂, occurs in cytoplasm, incomplete oxidation, produces 2 ATP, products ethanol+CO₂ (yeast) or lactic acid (muscles).

exercises.question: How is transportation in plants different from in humans?
exercises.answer: Plants: xylem (water+minerals upward), phloem (food bidirectional), no pump. Humans: heart pumps blood through arteries, veins, capillaries; oxygen transported by haemoglobin in RBCs; lymphatic system. Plants passive (osmosis, transpiration pull), humans active (pump+pressure).

exercises.question: Explain how glucose enters the leaf cells and how it is used for photosynthesis.
exercises.answer: CO₂ enters through stomata. Water absorbed by roots, transported by xylem to leaves. In chloroplasts, light energy absorbed by chlorophyll. Water split by light → H⁺ + O₂. CO₂ + H⁺ → glucose (Calvin cycle). O₂ released through stomata.

exercises.question: Why does blood not clot inside blood vessels but clots outside?
exercises.answer: Blood contains anticoagulants (heparin) inside vessels. When a vessel is damaged, platelets aggregate and a clotting cascade begins: prothrombin → thrombin → fibrinogen → fibrin mesh traps RBCs forming a clot. This prevents excessive blood loss.
